speaker: interviewer
text: Five, interview, interview with Bernard Godfryd.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, three seconds. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] do like another-- we'll try to do this like in another 10, 15 minutes of just talking. And then we can--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: --look at the photos. OK. Let's talk about the prisoner revolt now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Gusen. That was, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: The revolt that never came off.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 3? Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 5th, May 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 5?

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 5th, 1945, yes. Oh, God, what a day that was. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One night, we noticed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that most of the German hierarchy was gone in camp. And all they left were some guards in the towers were still there. But the following morning, they raised a Red Cross flag on a mast, right in the center of camp. It was kind of unusual. And of course, we were not going out to work for the last several days. We were not going out any longer. And of course, food was awful. The situation with food was terrible. There was hardly any bread left. And they gave us something like one thin soup a day, which was like hot water with some leaves floating, maybe spinach or something that resembled spinach. We couldn't even tell what it was-- some weeds, really. But, so we had a, an idea that it's coming to an end, towards an end. But of course, we didn't know. We didn't know. We already heard rumbling, artillery rumblings and-- from far away. And some of my Russian friends who knew about warfare told me-- kept saying that this is, this sounds like heavy howitzers, something like 25 kilometers away or maybe even closer. So we were hoping that maybe, maybe-- I mean, if, if the battle rages 25 kilometers away, it could come closer any day. It doesn't take much. And we were hoping just that any moment that something will happen. In the meantime, they brought in Austrian veterans of the First World War, armed them with those old vintage rifles, and, to replace the guards. And they were milling all over the place around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we also realized that those rifles were not loaded. We established that they had no ammunition, only rifles. Simply to keep us in place, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is when-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was May the 4th. May the 5th in the morning, May the 5th in the morning, Andre sent around his people to give out orders. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SOBBING] It's hard to talk about it. Even such a-- he was such an incredible man, such a spirit. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He really-- the way he carried this thing out was just amazing. But he had this-- he had these plans to take the control of the, of the camp. But it never came about, for the simple reason that May the 5th, around noon time or even earlier, something about 11 o'clock, people started-- people noticed tanks with a white star painted. We were sitting down, below a, a highway. The highway was maybe 200, 300 feet away from camp, way above us. And of course anything on the highway could have seen us very clearly. And apparently, there were two reconnaissance American tanks coming along that, the highway, driving towards Linz, north. And of course, they had no idea who we were or what we were. Apparently, they didn't know anything about our camp, that it even existed. They were two reconnaissance tanks, on their way to Linz. That was their, their, their mission. And suddenly, they notice people throwing their heads up in the air, going wild, jumping all over, running and screaming. And so they decided to turn off at some point, when they finally turned off. And they came to the gate. And at that point, I was already-- I mean, everybody was really up and around. And we already-- I mean, we knew and I-- probably with my nine English words, I was going to be the interpreter. And everybody-- Sasha and Andre and-- everybody was already at the gate, waiting for them when came around, the two tanks. And the first thing I know is the turret opens up. And there's this man with goggles and a helmet. And you can imagine what was happening. The gate is closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this is all barb wire, right? And of course, we can see through it. I mean there's visibility. And he must have been the head-- the, I know he had a bar. He was a lieutenant maybe or something like that. And he says-- the first thing he says is, who are you, what is this? What camp is this, who are you? And I said to him what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then another tank is, appears in this second reconnaissance tank. The, the, the driver sticks his head out on the front, through this, through that hatch. He opened the hatch, and he sticks his head out. His face is all dark from smog or smudge. And he sort of-- and at some point, while I am engaged talking to him, trying to explain-- not talking, really. Trying-- between my French and the few English words that I knew, I tell him what it's-- what it is and what we are doing here. And that, and I said to him, lots of sickness, I said. Lots of sickness, epidemics. And this fellow from the other tank looks at me. He was sort of closer to the gate. And he said to me, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] you are free now. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I said something in French, [FRENCH]. And everybody started screaming, [FRENCH]. And there must have been several hundred people at the gate. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, the emotion was just hard to, to recall. But it was hard to believe that this is possible, that we are really free. [SOBBING] If we could only get to-- I mean, I cannot tell you what it was like to see those, to see those tanks, full of mud. If we could only get to them. I mean, I'm sure everybody was ready to kiss them, to, to embrace them, to fall right over them. I-- people who haven't been able to move, sick people crawled on their bellies to the gate to see those tanks. It was like absolutely-- what a scene that was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just, awful-- I mean, not awful in the sense that-- it was simply an unbelievable scene. [SOBBING] I'll never forget what this meant to us, to be free again. It was something we, we were waiting for six years. Suddenly, this smudged face on a tank says that you are free now. And I, I didn't know the word free. But I knew what he meant. And I said, [FRENCH]. And this is really what he meant. This was really what the whole thing meant all about-- what the whole thing was about, to be free again. And it was just a indescribable moment I ever witnessed. I don't think I ever-- I think this is about the first time I talk about it. Because I, I wasn't even capable of writing as much about that moment as, as, I do-- as, as I talk now. It was just impossible. It was too emotional. And of course, the tanks had to get on their way, had to leave. And they did radio the rear echelons. And a couple hours later, Red Cross trucks were-- came in to deal with the sick. The American army sent in some personnel, medical personnel. And I decided then that I must leave. I cannot face this any longer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's people still dying left and right. There was a raging epidemic of dysentery going on for, for, for days or weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were left at camp at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were about, I would say about 4,000 people at the time, maybe a little more than 4,000. But I would say a good, a good 1,000 people were simply incapable of moving. And there must have been another several hundred dead, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all over the place. It was just a horrible, macabre scene. So it's like after an air raid. And those sick people who finally realized that they're free, they just believe it. They were moaning and calling their relatives in different languages. There was a Norwegian man, a teacher whom I knew, who was imprisoned. I knew him quite well. And he says-- at least, he said, I'll die a free man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spoke German to him. He spoke German. This is how we communicated. At least, he said, I'll die a free man. I couldn't face this. I couldn't stand this any more-- the dying, the, the death all over. I had to leave. And then when the, when the personnel came in, the medical personnel came in, they started to disinfect the area with DDT and everything else. The smell was awful. I, I was absolutely nauseated. And I wanted to find Andre. And I couldn't. He was gone. [SOBS] He was gone. Nobody was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I inquired. I ran all over the place. Where's Andre? Did you ever see Andre? And it seemed like nobody knew who I was talking about. They looked at me, like, strangely. Andre? [RUSSIAN] Andre, [RUSSIAN] Andre.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were Russians. I said, Andre. Andre-- well, somebody said to me, there are two Andres in my barrack. I said no, Andre from block five. [RUSSIAN]. Nobody knew Andre. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is when actually-- this is probably, this was the time when I realized what freedom means, what freedom meant. To experience a moment like this is absolutely beyond anybody's belief if these things are possible. The meaning and the significance takes on such tremendous force. It is absolutely-- it catapults you. It's sort of, you're almost ready to jump out your skin. This is kind of a feeling I had. And all I know is that I wanted to leave. I must leave this place. I can't stand it anymore, just to be out in the open. Because in order to believe that you're free, you must be out of here. I couldn't stand the sight of the barbed wires or the gate or anything else. Even when the American personnel came in with their Red Cross trucks and everything else, to me, it was still uniforms. I must get away from it. I don't want to see any uniforms. I don't want to see any barbed wire. I just want to see a little sky and, and trees, just out, out. And I did. And this is, of course, the following day, I was in Linz. Oh, the war was practically over. This was May the-- the following day was May the 6th. I was in Linz. And of course, my first encounter was the [? Gertners. ?] The German couple, the Austrian couple, sitting. I was just walking, aimlessly. This was like the suburbs of Linz. And I walked around aimlessly. And I see this-- this is May the 6th, springtime. It was a beautiful day. And I walked down this narrow street. And there was a row of little one-family homes. Looked like they were there a long time, old homes-- gardens, front yards. You could see the first flowers had been coming up. And, and I stopped to look. Because suddenly it struck me like my grandparents in the country, sitting on the, on the porch. This is how I pictured-- this is how I remembered them. And I stood there, looking at them. And apparently, the man noticed me. And he says to me-- and he greeted me. [GERMAN]. This was the greeting they had, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: And I responded. And he says to me, where do you come from? And I don't remember exactly what I answered.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't going to tell him I just came out of camp or something. And I said to him, oh, from Linz, I think I did-- from Linz. Come on over, he says, he invited me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked around through the yard. And I-- he invited me to sit down, next to them. There was a chair, a stool. And I sit down. And this is how we started. This is how we hit it off. And I told him where I come from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we sat there for the longest time, talking. This was already afternoon and getting to be afternoon. And they asked me to join them in a little supper that they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: There wasn't much, but it was a very good soup and some bread they had. She even offered me a cookie. I was amazed, a cookie. What does it taste like? I haven't had a cookie in a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the whole encounter was very, very, extremely interesting to me. It was a test of freedom. I'm able to talk to other people. People are talking to me. I hear language. Somebody addresses me sir, not [GERMAN]. That's kind of unusual. And slowly, my-- I was getting adjusted to the sounds and to the sights and, and friendly people. I mean, I was simply-- here, I know. I was quite aware of the fact I'm still in Austria, enemy territory. But still, there are some nice people here. You know, they asked me in. They share their food with, with me and all that. Not only that, but eventually, I realized it's past curfew time. I cannot go back to Linz. And she says, don't worry. She said, we have plenty of room here. You can stay over in my, in our son's room, you know, sure enough, which I do. To my regret, [LAUGHS] I found this to be a horrible, horrifying experience. This son of his is-- there's a picture of him hanging, in an SS uniform. And this man told me earlier that his two sons were in the army, the Wehrmacht. The Wehrmacht and the SS are two different things.

speaker: interviewer
text: God, I thought to myself. God, this man was really lying to me, wasn't he? This man, this-- I said, you know, what a face that was. And what do we do? I mean, I wasn't going to walk out and take a chance and walk after curfew. Even though it was American occupied, I wasn't ready to deal with it. Plus, you get stopped by a patrol or anything. God knows, somebody might be trigger happy and shoot you right, right through your head. Who knows? It's war time.

speaker: interviewee
text: War was still on, officially. Officially, the war didn't end until the 8th. And this is May the 6th.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided to agree. I decided to stay overnight in this room. And they were very, they were very nice about it. It's a very nice room, with some very old antique furniture.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made myself at home. And sure enough, there was a little night table next to the bed I was going to sleep in. And, I don't know, on impulse, I just pulled this drawer, little drawer out. I don't know why. Just-- And sure enough, there's a packet of letters, tied with a ribbon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I pulled a letter out from the middle, and I nearly fainted. Well, not yet-- until I saw the letter. But the letter was [GERMAN], and I realized it was somewhere inside Russia. I opened the letter, I pulled the letter out. His son Kurt wrote to them. And the first thing he says, the first sentence he says is that I hope you will be proud of me for the work I am doing here. And he describes what he's doing. And I was absolutely horrified.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the [GERMAN] commanders, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] executing people. And he tells the parents how proud they should be of him. There will be a new order in Europe now, he says, and that, that the fuhrer will put them on a new level, and all that. And he talks about-- he talked about the fact that he couldn't-- that he was trying to contact his cousin. But apparently, he, he wasn't able to contact the cousin, Hans, who was supposed to be in a certain place, but he wasn't. But still, he's going to write to him that they shouldn't worry, that he's in good hands, that Hans is OK, and that they shouldn't worry about him, too. Because his outfit is now doing some mopping up. Apparently, he was not-- he was not on the fighting front. He was behind the front, eliminating-- executing people. This is what the job was. Yes, [GERMAN] commanders. Cleaning up the area from Jews, gypsies, and undesirable elements.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, you can imagine what-- how shocked I was when I read this letter. I put it back in place. And I couldn't sleep all night. And for some reason, I imagined that this guy must be alive somewhere, that he might show up any minute here and see me in his bed. He might simply do away with me, you know? Suddenly, I got frightened over this whole aspect as to who his son is. And now that the war is over, I thought maybe he's hiding someplace. Maybe he's somewhere around the neighborhood, who knows? I didn't sleep all night, not a wink. And eventually, I decided to leave as soon as I could, to leave that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And early morning, very early mornings, I heard a knock on the door. It was Herr [? Gertner, ?] wanted to let me know that he and his wife are going out to stand in a line to get bread, that they will be back shortly, so whatever-- that I'm alone, that I'm alone in the house. And I said, OK, I will be getting up soon, I said too. And sure enough-- and then I thought, it will be a good time for me to get out of there while they were gone. And that's what I did. And eventually, well, I saw them through the window. They left. And a few minutes later, I was ready to leave. And I went downstairs. And there was this hallway, and there was a dining room to the left. And the door was open. And I looked in. And I, I noticed a candle holder in a little-- like, a little alcove. And there was a little table. And on the table, stood a candle holder, a silver candle holder, identical-- identical of the ones that my grandmother had. I knew it was-- came from a Jewish home. It had to. This was not a candle holder you could-- a non-Jew would buy, with grape leaves. And of course I was shocked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I thought to myself, why one? There must be another one. You know, usually they come in, they come in pairs. Sure enough, I found the other one too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to the other candle holder in the little break front, I found a passover cup, a silver cup, a passover cup with inscription. I was absolutely-- then I realized. And I knew where, where this all, where all this came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: They must, they had to be aware of all this. Kurt must have shipped it to them or brought it home on his furlough or something. A souvenir, war trophies, you know? They all had it. A lot of them did, you know? Jewish homes-- they robbed and stole. And eventually brought it home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look-- I remember in 1945, I was in Berlin, after the war was over. This was Christmastime, wintertime '45. I was traveling through Berlin from Poland. And there was on Alexanderplatz, a very famous square. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were Germans, selling all kinds of things-- personal items, gold rings, whatever. Because they were, they, they had it tough at the time. There was not much food around. And they really had to do something about it. So they were out there selling their jewelry and all that, whatever they could. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was out there with somebody I knew from Radom. And he told me about a story. A week earlier, somebody found his mother's wedding band. A German was selling his mother's wedding band.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know it was his mother's? The engraving-- her name and the date of the marriage. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And apparently, he made a big fuss. He asked the German where he got, where he got this from. He bought it from him. He asked him where he got it from. And he said that he got it from a neighbor who asked him to sell it. So apparently, he asked him to take him to this neighbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: The neighbor happened to be a widow, an old lady, in her, I don't know, 50s, 60s, or whatever. And she said that her son gave it to her a couple years ago. Where was her son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was in the army. He got killed on the Russian front. But her son gave it to her, with a few other items like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did, where did her son get it from? Took it off Jewish hands, Jewish fingers, Jewish homes. They robbed and stole whatever they could. You can imagine [SIGHS] how these things-- After the war, your parents were dead. But you found your brother and you found your sister. And then you were in, I know, Stuttgart, Germany for a while, in the DP camp. And you were actually helping prisoners be smuggled, weren't you, to Palestine? Weren't you helping--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these were not prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, not prisoners anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: We aren't talking about, we are, no, no. These were not prisoner. We are free. I mean, after the war is over, we are free.

words.text: mean, after the war is over, we are free.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're free. But you were helping them get, like, forged papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: You should do away, you should do away with this word, prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're right. It was the wrong word.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are not prisoners. We are free then. We were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're free.

speaker: interviewee
text: What, what I was doing at the time was simply helping to get people who had intentions of going to Palestine, survivors who lived in Austria or Germany. Especially, I was interested in getting people out of Austria to Germany. And then eventually, I helped to get people from Germany to France. So that they could get closer to the ports of embarkation. Like, Marseilles, or from France was easier for them to get to Italy, maybe, whatever. But there was a big-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was a big concentration of survivors in France, who were being shipped to Palestine illegally. You realize this was a time when the British blockaded the, the Mediterranean. To get through was not easy on a boat. And of course, there were times they would [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stop a boat with illegal immigrants. They would take them to Cyprus and keep them in internment camps. That was a rough time a lot of those people had. These camps are not much better from the camps the Germans had. Well, in some sense. They were not killing them, but they were really mistreating them. And I did some of that, yes. Well, I have some good contacts. When I lived in Germany, I got to know some officers of the French Air Force, in an occupied part of Germany by France, around Friedrichshafen. I got to know some very incredible people, who were willing to help the [NON-ENGLISH], to help people emigrate to Palestine. And I even got some people-- I set them in contact with, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with some people who were instrumental in getting weapons in Europe. And I think they got quite a few things out of those, out of that source-- captured, German captured weapons, which were controlled at that time by the French occupation forces. So that was a good opportunity at the time, while I was able to, to help along.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: In June of '47, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I guess we'll kind of quickly go into your marriage and your work at as a photographer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this came later. June '47, when I first arrived, I, I decided to go to school. I could not go full time, because I had no-- I had to work, of course. I did take some courses and wherever I could for the journalism, photography, et cetera. Between the New School and School of Visual Arts, wherever I was able to squeeze in a course, which helped me. And I did work at a photography studio in New York City for a while, until I was drafted in the army. I was drafted in January, '49. And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did the army. And while I was in the service, I had an opportunity once to photograph General Omar Bradley, who came down to Fort Benning in Georgia for some special occasion. This was the time when magazine was trying to get him, because he needed a picture of him for an upcoming overseas addition, a cover shot. And they couldn't get to him because he was in Fort Benning, tied up. And I was-- photographers were not allowed into Fort Benning at that time, to photograph this whole situation. So the Atlanta Bureau Chief of approached me, because I was assigned to do the job for the service, for the army. I was the photographer then, official front photographer for the army. And the whole Pentagon was down there, not only Bradley, everybody. The Secretary of Defense, Johnson, everybody was there. And this man from-- the Atlanta Bureau Chief from approached me and asked me-- when he saw me with a camera-- and asked me what I could possibly take a cover shot of General Bradley for I didn't know what was. I'd never heard of it. [LAUGHS] I said, OK, whatever it is. And he said to me, well, listen. We will even pay you for it, so don't worry. You know, that's great. I said, as soon as I get a chance. And I looked around. And of course, there was the whole top brass was there, everybody. And, well, I was a, I already had photographed generals at that time. And I was around top brass and all that, but not that top, not the Pentagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how your career began at

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is when-- eventually I walked up the bleachers where General Bradley was sitting. And I went over to him, and I told him here is a man from who wants me to take a photo of you, sir, for the overseas edition. And he said to me, well, go right ahead. And this is how it all started. And this picture was the catalyst of my getting a job at and my lining up. And eventually, when I came out of the service, I was freelancing for them. And some years later, I would say about five years later, they put me on staff.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on staff for 30 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 37 Years.

speaker: interviewer
text: 37 Years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want-- because--
